quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Work"?

options.text: F_c = mv²/r = mrω²; the net radial force required to maintain circular motion at a given radius and speed
W = ∫F·dr; the scalar integral of force along displacement; measured in joules (J); positive when force and displacement are in the same direction
I = ∫r² dm; requires integration over the mass distribution of the object
τ = -mgd sinθ for a pendulum; for small θ, τ ≈ -mgdθ, giving angular SHM with ω = √(mgd/I)

quiz_questions.explanation: "Work" is defined as: W = ∫F·dr; the scalar integral of force along displacement; measured in joules (J); positive when force and displacement are in the same direction. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Kinetic energy"?

options.text: F = Gm₁m₂/r²; every pair of masses attracts each other with a force proportional to the product of masses and inversely proportional to the square of distance
x(t) = x₀ + ∫v(t)dt; integrating velocity recovers position, with the constant of integration set by initial conditions
KE = ½mv²; the energy of motion; changes when net work is done on the object
½mv₁² - GMm/r₁ = ½mv₂² - GMm/r₂; used to find speed at any orbital distance without knowing forces

quiz_questions.explanation: "Kinetic energy" is defined as: KE = ½mv²; the energy of motion; changes when net work is done on the object. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Work-Energy Theorem"?

options.text: sinθ ≈ θ (in radians) for small angles; this linearizes the restoring torque and produces SHM
W_net = ΔKE; the total work done by all forces equals the change in kinetic energy
x(t) = x₀ + ∫v(t)dt; integrating velocity recovers position, with the constant of integration set by initial conditions
A·B = ABcosθ = AxBx + AyBy; a scalar measuring the projection of one vector onto another; used in calculating work

quiz_questions.explanation: "Work-Energy Theorem" is defined as: W_net = ΔKE; the total work done by all forces equals the change in kinetic energy. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Conservative force"?

options.text: x(t) = x₀ + ∫v(t)dt; integrating velocity recovers position, with the constant of integration set by initial conditions
F_c = mv²/r = mrω²; the net radial force required to maintain circular motion at a given radius and speed
A force whose work depends only on initial and final positions, not the path taken (e.g., gravity, spring force); associated with potential energy
F_avg = Δp/Δt = J/Δt; the constant force that would deliver the same impulse over the same time interval

quiz_questions.explanation: "Conservative force" is defined as: A force whose work depends only on initial and final positions, not the path taken (e.g., gravity, spring force); associated with potential energy. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Potential energy"?

options.text: sinθ ≈ θ (in radians) for small angles; this linearizes the restoring torque and produces SHM
Energy stored due to position or configuration; gravitational PE = mgh (near Earth's surface) and elastic PE = ½kx²
L = Iω; the rotational analog of linear momentum, measured in kg·m²/s
f_s ≤ μ_s N; opposes the tendency of motion between surfaces in contact; self-adjusting up to the maximum value μ_s N

quiz_questions.explanation: "Potential energy" is defined as: Energy stored due to position or configuration; gravitational PE = mgh (near Earth's surface) and elastic PE = ½kx². The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "W = ∫F·dr; the scalar integral of force along displacement; measured in joules (J); positive when force and displacement are in the same direction"?

options.text: Work-Energy Theorem
Unit vector
Work
Vertical circle dynamics

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Work".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "KE = ½mv²; the energy of motion; changes when net work is done on the object"?

options.text: Newton's First Law
Center of mass (discrete)
Angular frequency
Kinetic energy

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Kinetic energy".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "W_net = ΔKE; the total work done by all forces equals the change in kinetic energy"?

options.text: Torque-angular momentum relation
Total energy in orbit
Work-Energy Theorem
Energy dissipation

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Work-Energy Theorem".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "A force whose work depends only on initial and final positions, not the path taken (e.g., gravity, spring force); associated with potential energy"?

options.text: Conservative force
Gravitational field
Angular momentum (particle)
Vertical circle dynamics

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Conservative force".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Energy stored due to position or configuration; gravitational PE = mgh (near Earth's surface) and elastic PE = ½kx²"?

options.text: Simple pendulum period
Potential energy
Kinetic friction
Coefficient of friction

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Potential energy".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Work and Energy is accurate?

options.text: Power and Efficiency describe the rate of energy transfer and the fraction of input energy converted to useful output.
Conservation of Energy states that in an isolated system, total mechanical energy (KE + PE) remains constant when only conservative forces do work: KE₁ + PE₁ = KE₂ + PE₂.
Work and Energy provide a scalar framework for analyzing motion without tracking forces and accelerations over time.
Free-body diagrams identify all forces acting on an object, enabling systematic application of F = ma in each coordinate direction.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Work and Energy as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Work and Energy?

options.text: Work is defined as W = ∫F·dr (the dot product integral of force along the displacement path), and the Work-Energy Theorem states W_net = ΔKE = ½mv² - ½mv₀².
Dynamics connects force and motion through Newton's three laws.
Any vector can be decomposed into perpendicular components (typically x and y), enabling independent analysis of motion and forces in each direction.
AP Physics C emphasizes setting up and evaluating these integrals.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Work and Energy as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Work and Energy?

options.text: Universal Gravitation states that every mass attracts every other mass with force F = Gm₁m₂/r², where G = 6.674 × 10⁻¹¹ N·m²/kg².
The gravitational field g = GM/r² at distance r from a mass M gives the local acceleration due to gravity, explaining why g varies with altitude and location on Earth.
AP Physics C emphasizes calculus-based kinematics, using derivatives and integrals to describe motion with non-constant acceleration.
Conservative forces (gravity, springs) have associated potential energy functions, while non-conservative forces (friction) convert mechanical energy to thermal energy.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Work and Energy as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Work and Energy?

options.text: Work and Energy provide a scalar framework for analyzing motion without tracking forces and accelerations over time.
Circular Motion occurs when an object follows a curved path, requiring a centripetal (center-seeking) acceleration a_c = v²/r directed radially inward.
This inverse-square law governs planetary orbits, satellite motion, and tidal effects.
Conservation of angular momentum explains phenomena from spinning ice skaters to the formation of galaxies.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Work and Energy as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Work and Energy?

options.text: Work and Kinetic Energy defines work rigorously using calculus: W = ∫F·dr, the line integral of force along the displacement path.
Work is defined as W = ∫F·dr (the dot product integral of force along the displacement path), and the Work-Energy Theorem states W_net = ΔKE = ½mv² - ½mv₀².
Multiple-force problems require careful free-body analysis at specific positions.
Wave properties—wavelength λ, frequency f, speed v = λf, and amplitude—are described mathematically by y(x,t) = A sin(kx - ωt), linking oscillation parameters to wave propagation.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Work and Energy as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Work and Energy?

options.text: Engineering design of crash barriers using the work-energy theorem to calculate the stopping force needed to safely decelerate vehicles of known mass and speed
Engineering spring-loaded mechanisms (car bumpers, mattresses) by calculating work stored and released using W = ½kx²
Earthquake engineering analyzing seismic wave propagation (P-waves longitudinal, S-waves transverse) through different geological layers
Wind turbine design optimizing blade angular velocity to maximize energy capture while maintaining safe centripetal acceleration at blade tips

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Work and Energy.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Work and Energy?

options.text: Automotive suspension system analysis modeling shock absorbers as damped SHM to optimize ride comfort and handling characteristics
Sports biomechanics analyzing the impulse delivered by a bat to a baseball using force-time graphs from high-speed sensors
Calculating energy requirements for elevators and conveyor systems using work against gravity W = mgh
Calculating the angular deceleration of a flywheel when braking force is applied to determine stopping time and number of revolutions

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Work and Energy.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Work and Energy applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Automotive engineering placing a vehicle's center of mass low and centrally to optimize handling, stability, and rollover resistance
Wrench design and usage: longer handles provide greater torque for the same applied force, following τ = rF
Analyzing bungee jump physics using conservation of energy between gravitational PE and elastic PE to determine maximum stretch and maximum speed
Engineering spring-loaded mechanisms (car bumpers, mattresses) by calculating work stored and released using W = ½kx²

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Work and Energy.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Work and Energy?

options.text: Athletic performance analysis measuring power output of cyclists and runners to optimize training and predict race performance
Stellar evolution: collapsing gas clouds spin faster as they shrink (I decreases → ω increases), explaining the rapid rotation of neutron stars
Engineering design of crash barriers using the work-energy theorem to calculate the stopping force needed to safely decelerate vehicles of known mass and speed
Analyzing asteroid capture: determining whether an approaching asteroid's total energy is negative enough to become bound to a planet's gravitational field

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Work and Energy.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Work and Energy in practice?

options.text: Bridge engineering calculating torques about supports to determine forces on beams and ensure structural equilibrium
Calculating energy requirements for elevators and conveyor systems using work against gravity W = mgh
Figure skating spins: pulling arms in reduces I, increasing ω to conserve angular momentum (demonstrating I's physical significance)
Sports equipment design (golf clubs, tennis rackets) optimizing mass ratios and coefficients of restitution for maximum energy transfer to the ball

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Work and Energy.
